Critical AS, CAD, NSTEMI [**1-21**] & [**2-19**] c angioplasty of LAD.

CHF, EF-25%, HTN, hyperlipidemia, squamos cell CA on right medial calf, chronic venous stasis, hypothyroidism, depression, UTI's, rectal ulcers, h/o c-diff, and colon adenoma '[**41**].  Recent admission for acute influenza PNA c/b hypotension.
DNR/DNI.
"I am on the [**Location (un) **] now, right?"

No c/o pain, MAE. OOB to chair c 2 assists, minimal weight bearing.
Asympotmatic c SBP in 80's.
DP's weak palp.
No c/o CP.
Mag 1.9, rec'd 2gm IV.
Resp: Lungs clear with crackles ~[**12-16**] way up.
No c/o SOB.
Congested cough.
4L np, sats 95-100. RR 15-27, Noted sleep apnea.

Takes pills whole in applesauce.
Tolerating meds.
BS present, no BM.
GU: Lasix gtt @ 2.5mg/hr.

UO 80-200cc/hr. -750cc for day, -1.2L for LOS.

RLE dressing changed.
Wound c yellow base and pink egdes.
Slight serous drainage.
WTD dressing applied.
Afebrile.
PLAN: CXR, continue to monitor response to Lasix gtt.
Goal met..?hold gtt.
Advance activity and diet as tol, ?PT consult.
Change leg dressing ?wound care consult.
Discuss transfer plans with CCU team on rounds.
Emotionally support pt and family and keep updated on plan of care.

.    # Hypoxia  Patient has worsening respiratory status; continues to    require face mask and NC O2.
Patient has RSV PNA, idiopathic pulmonary    fibrosis, and pulmonary edema.
Despite Echo, patient has CHF and this    can be contributing to the pulmonary edema.
Working diagnosis includes    fluid overload on top of underlying lung disease in setting of afib    with RVR and loss of atrial 10% forward flow.
Patient has been    diuresied with Lasix 40mg IV BID.

Patient was on continue vanco and    ceftriaxone, has GPCs in pair and clusters on sputum, unclear if this    is source of fever because he continues to spike despite coverage, but    stopped Abx [**1-14**] since RSV positive.
Patient placed on droplet    precautions.
AFB negative x3, no concern for TB.    - diurese with Lasix 40mg IV BID until renal effects noted    .    # Fevers  unclear etiology, PNA v. bronchitis v. SBP.
IR did    diagnostic paracentesis and no evidence of SBP.
- pan cultured again    - f/u blood, sputum, stool, urine    .    # Afib with RVR.
Continues to be in afib but well rate controlled on    dilt, could be cause of poor heart function causing pulmonary edema.
Patient is on max dose Dilt 90mg PO QID and continues to have episodes    of RVR, started metoprolol 12.5mg PO BID on [**1-14**].
TSH elevated,    considered to be sick euthyroid.
Echo showed normal ejection fraction    and no evidence of structural heart disease.
- cont dilt 90 mg qid    - consider anticoagulation in the long run    .    # Relative hypotension.
To SBP 90s after receiving 1 IV dilaudid and    IV/PO metoprolol with Afib/RVR in addition.

Now remaining in the 90s    up to 100s now, but tolerating well, likely from diltiazem treatment,    continues to be in 90s-100s    - likely in setting of nodal blockade for afib    - no change in mentation, or change in UO, perfusing appropriately    - titrate dilt to SBP of approximately 100    .    # chronic pain disorder  started tramadol on [**1-14**].
Patient has been on    IV dilaudid chronically.
.    # GIB: stable, CT showed colitis at the hepatic flexure to rectosigmoid    junction.
DDx includes ischemic, however large vessels were patent in    prelim read of CT.
Infectious suggestive on CT scan and inflammatory    also on the differential.
GI following.
No transfusions required with    continued stable hematocrit, although trending downward steadily.
- stable, now checking only daily hcts    - continue to trend Hct, tx for hct <25    - IV PPI [**Hospital1 **]    - active type and cross    - monitor hemodynamically and maintain access.
.    #Colitis: see above.
Possible ischemic vs infectious, although in    setting afib more likely ischemic.
Further workup did not support this    and the abdominal pain appears to be from chronic pain disorder.
- colonoscopy as inpatient v. outpatient to evaluate per GI    - stopping antibiotics    - c.diff and other stool cultures neg    - ADAT, NPO if respiratory status worsens      .

# Hep C infection: Pt states he has a history of hep c.    - HCV VL 3 million    - liver on CT scan is unremarkable    - likely cause of elevated INR, not actively bleeding, does not need to    be reversed    .
# h/o CAD: CP c/w chronic angina symptoms.
EKG without any acute    changes except had demand ischemia when in afib with high rates, repeat    EKGs were normal.
Patient completed ROMI.
- on ASA.

Pericardial effusion.

Height: (in) 72 Weight (lb): 212 BSA (m2): 2.19 m2 BP (mm Hg): 167/74 HR (bpm): 92 Status: Inpatient Date/Time: [**2196-12-6**] at 14:10 Test: Portable TTE (Complete) Doppler: Complete pulse and color flow Contrast: None Technical Quality: Adequate
INTERPRETATION:
Findings:
Only parasternal and subcostal views available.
LEFT ATRIUM: The left atrium is normal in size.
RIGHT ATRIUM/INTERATRIAL SEPTUM: No atrial septal defect is seen by 2D or color Doppler.
LEFT VENTRICLE: Left ventricular wall thicknesses and cavity size are normal.
There is moderate regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction.

LV WALL MOTION: The following resting regional left ventricular wall motion abnormalities are seen: mid anterior - akinetic; mid anteroseptal - akinetic; anterior apex - hypokinetic; septal apex- akinetic; apex - akinetic;
RIGHT VENTRICLE: Right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are normal.
AORTIC VALVE: The aortic valve leaflets appear structurally normal with good leaflet excursion.
No aortic regurgitation is seen.
MITRAL VALVE: The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened.
There is moderate mitral annular calcification.
No mitral regurgitation is seen.
TRICUSPID VALVE: The tricuspid valve leaflets are normal.
Mild tricuspid [1+] regurgitation is seen.
PERICARDIUM: There is a small to moderate sized pericardial effusion.
The effusion appears circumferential.
GENERAL COMMENTS: Based on [**2189**] AHA endocarditis prophylaxis recommendations, the echo findings indicate a low risk (prophylaxis not recommended).
Clinical decisions regarding the need for prophylaxis should be based on clinical and echocardiographic data.
The echocardiographic results were reviewed by telephone with the houseofficer caring for the patient.
Conclusions: The left atrium is normal in size.
Left ventricular wall thicknesses and cavity size are normal.
There is moderate regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with focal akinesis of the distal half of the anterior septum and anterior wall and apex.
The inferior wall and basal anterior walls are not adequately seen.
The distal lateral wall is hypokinetic.
Right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are normal.
The aortic valve leaflets appear structurally normal with good leaflet excursion.
The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened.
There is a small to moderate sized circumferential pericardial effusion without evidence of RV diastolic collapse.
The effusion extends 1.4cm around the right atrium, 1.0 cm anterior to the right ventricle, 1.5 cm around the LV apex and lateral wall.
IMPRESSION: Regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction c/w CAD.
Mild-moderate circumferential pericardial effusion.
Based on [**2189**] AHA endocarditis prophylaxis recommendations, the echo findings indicate a low risk (prophylaxis not recommended).

  [**Hospital 2**] MEDICAL CONDITION:   58 year old woman with endocarditis requiring PICC, failed regular picc   placement and needs IR guidance  REASON FOR THIS EXAMINATION:   IR placement of PICC  
                                  FINAL REPORT  INDICATION:  58-year-old woman with endocarditis requiring PICC, failed  regular PICC placement and needs IR guidance.

PHYSICIANS:  Dr. [**First Name4 (NamePattern1) 490**] [**Last Name (NamePattern1) **] Mhuircheartaigh (radiology fellow) supervised by  Dr. [**First Name8 (NamePattern2) 147**] [**Last Name (NamePattern1) 148**] (radiology attending).
RADIATION:  1.6 minutes fluoroscopy time, 12 mGy.
PROCEDURE DETAILS:  The patient was brought to the Angiography Suite and  placed supine on the table.
An initial limited ultrasound of both upper  extremities demonstrated that the brachial vein and artery were patent in the  right arm, but the basilic vein was occluded.
On the left side, both the  basilic and brachial veins were patent; therefore, we elected to target the  left basilic vein.
This skin of the left upper arm was prepped and draped in  usual sterile fashion.
Using ultrasound guidance, an appropriate target was  selected, approximately 4 cc of 1% lidocaine was infiltrated into the skin and  subcutaneous tissues for local anesthesia.
Again using ultrasound guidance, a  micropuncture needle was advanced into the left basilic vein.
A nitinol wire  was advanced via the needle up to the central veins.
It was not possible to  pass the nitinol wire down into the IVC despite multiple attempts.
The  micropuncture needle was removed following a small skin [**Doctor Last Name **] and exchanged for  a 4.5fr peel away sheath.
The length of catheter needed was measured at 31 cm  and the catheter was cut; however, when this was advanced, this was too short  and lay within the proximal SVC/left brachiocephalic vein.
Therefore, this  catheter was removed and a new Power PICC was selected, this was cut to 34.5  cm.
A new 4.5 French peel-away sheath was advanced over the wire and the  catheter was advanced over the wire via the sheath.
The peel-away sheath was  removed, at the completion of the procedure, the tip of the PICC lay in the  mid-to-distal SVC.
The catheter was aspirated and flushed without difficulty.
The catheter was secured to the skin with a StatLock device and sterile  Tegaderm was applied.
There were no immediate post-procedure complications.
IMPRESSION:  Successful placement of a single-lumen Power PICC with the tip in  the mid SVC.  The catheter was flushed and is ready for use.
(Over)
